vision_statement: Decifer will be a cloud-hosted, profitable automated trading bot that friends and family of the founder can access through a web interface, connecting their own broker accounts with configurable risk profiles. The path runs through proving profitability on paper first, then live single-account, then cloud-hosted multi-user scale.
current_stage_assessment: Decifer is in paper_trading_single_account stage and has shipped meaningful infrastructure in the last 24 hours: a 10-dimension signal pipeline, drawdown brake, ATR trailing stop, daily loss limit, intraday adaptive modes, and signal pipeline extraction from bot.py. The core signal and risk scaffolding is maturing rapidly. However, 70 tests are failing, the candle gate is broken, and agent JSON parse failures indicate fragile LLM output handling — these are reliability gaps that must be closed before paper trading results can be trusted enough to justify moving toward live trading.
executive_summary: Decifer has had an extremely productive 24-hour sprint — 10-dimension signals, drawdown brakes, HWM persistence, ATR trailing stops, and regime-locked VIX proxy are all live. The codebase is growing fast (25,730 lines, 37 modules) and the test suite has expanded to 1,332 passing tests, but 70 are failing including a fully broken candle gate module and recurring agent JSON parse errors that would cause silent trade execution failures in production. The most urgent need is stabilising what has just been built: fix the failing tests, harden agent output parsing, and then validate that the new 10-dimension signal pipeline is actually producing better signal quality than the previous 9-dimension version before layering more features on top. The backlog contains 77 active features — many are duplicates or overlapping (HMM appears 4+ times, IC weighting appears twice, walk-forward appears twice) — and a housekeeping pass to deduplicate is overdue. The roadmap is sound but the gap between current stage and live trading readiness requires a focused stabilisation phase rather than continued feature addition.

recommended_actions.action: Fix 7 failing candle gate tests and resolve agent JSON parse errors in agents.py
recommended_actions.why_now: The candle gate tests are 100% failing, meaning either the gate implementation is broken or the test contract has drifted from the implementation after the 10-dimension signal refactor. Agent JSON parse errors ('Sorry, no recommendation.', empty string, 'not json at all') are logged at ERROR level and represent a silent execution risk — if an agent returns malformed JSON during a live paper trade, the consensus pipeline may degrade or skip a guard. These must be fixed before any confidence in paper trading results is valid. This is the single highest priority item.
recommended_actions.source: Test Health: test_candle_gate.py — 7 failures; Test Health: agents.py:483 JSON parse errors
recommended_actions.cowork_prompt:
SESSION BRIEF: Fix failing candle gate tests and harden agent JSON parse handling in Decifer.

CONTEXT: Decifer is a paper-trading bot. Yesterday's sprint included a major refactor — signal pipeline extracted from bot.py into signal_pipeline.py, and a 10-dimension signal refactor (alpha-pipeline-v2 commit). After this refactor, 7 candle gate tests are failing and agent JSON parse errors are being logged at ERROR level.

FAILING TESTS:
- tests/test_candle_gate.py::TestCandleGateBlocking::test_buy_blocked_without_candle
- tests/test_candle_gate.py::TestCandleGateBlocking::test_sell_blocked_without_candle
- tests/test_candle_gate.py::TestCandleGateBlocking::test_score_preserved_when_blocked
- tests/test_candle_gate.py::TestCandleGateBlocking::test_direction_becomes_neutral_when_blocked
- tests/test_candle_gate.py::TestCandleGateAllowing::test_buy_passes_with_bull_candle
- tests/test_candle_gate.py::TestCandleGateAllowing::test_sell_passes_with_bear_candle
- tests/test_candle_gate.py::TestCandleGateAllowing::test_candle_bonus_still_added_to_score

AGENT ERRORS: In agents.py around line 483, the final agent JSON parse is raising errors on: 'Sorry, no recommendation.', empty string, and 'not json at all'. These are not being handled gracefully.

TASKS:
1. Run the failing candle gate tests and inspect the actual vs expected output. Determine if the gate's interface changed in the signal_pipeline.py extraction and update either the implementation or the tests to match the correct contract.
2. In agents.py at the JSON parse block (around line 483), add a robust fallback: if JSON parse fails after retries, return a typed NullRecommendation object (ABSTAIN direction, 0 confidence, reason='parse_failure') rather than raising or silently dropping. Add a test for each of the three failure strings shown in the error log.
3. Run the full test suite and confirm 0 failures in candle gate and 0 unhandled parse errors.
4. Confirm test count is at least 1332 passing with fewer than 63 failures (net improvement).

recommended_actions.action: Validate that the 10-dimension signal pipeline (alpha-pipeline-v2) produces measurably better signal quality than the previous 9-dimension version
recommended_actions.why_now: The 10-dimension signal refactor was committed today and is now the live signal engine. Before any more signal features are layered on top (IC weighting, regime-conditional routing, PEAD, UOA, etc.), there must be a baseline check that the new pipeline is performing at least as well as the old one. If the refactor introduced a regression — wrong dimension weights, broken normalisation, or a scoring bug — then all subsequent paper trade results will be invalid and all downstream features will be built on a broken foundation. This is the prerequisite for everything signal-related.
recommended_actions.source: Recent Commits: feat(signals): alpha-pipeline-v2 — 10-dimension signal refactor; Research: Signal Quality & Alpha Generation (IC-weighted dynamic signal scoring)
recommended_actions.cowork_prompt:
SESSION BRIEF: Validate the alpha-pipeline-v2 10-dimension signal refactor in Decifer.

CONTEXT: Today's commit 'feat(signals): alpha-pipeline-v2 — 10-dimension signal refactor' upgraded signals.py from a 9-dimension to a 10-dimension signal composite. This is now the live signal engine for all paper trades. Before building further on top of it, we need to verify it is working correctly.

TASKS:
1. Identify what the 10th dimension is — compare the current signals.py signal list against the 9 documented dimensions (the 9th was REVERSION, recently shipped). Document what was added and why.
2. Run the backtester against a fixed test window (e.g., last 60 days of paper data or historical data if available) using both the old and new signal composite. Compare: average signal score distribution, number of trades triggered, any obviously broken scoring (NaN, all-zero, out-of-range values).
3. Check that each of the 10 dimensions is actually contributing non-zero scores across a sample of 20 candidate stocks. If any dimension is always 0 or always max, flag it as a calibration bug.
4. Check Z-score normalisation if it was applied in this commit — confirm that the composite score distribution is centered and not dominated by one dimension.
5. Write a brief validation report (can be a markdown file in /docs or a comment in the PR): what the 10 dimensions are, sample score distributions, and a pass/fail verdict on the refactor quality.
6. If regressions are found, fix them before declaring the pipeline stable.

recommended_actions.action: Deduplicate and consolidate overlapping pipeline features: HMM (4 entries), IC weighting (2 entries), Walk-Forward (2 entries), Connection Manager (2 entries), Per-Account Config (2 entries)
recommended_actions.why_now: The active pipeline has 77 items and the backlog has 25, but a significant portion are duplicates or near-duplicates: HMM Regime Detection appears at least 4 times under different names, IC-Weighted Dynamic Signal Scoring appears twice with near-identical descriptions, Walk-Forward Backtesting and Walk-Forward WFE Validator are listed separately, Connection Manager appears in both active and backlog, Per-Account Config Profiles appears in both active and backlog. This creates planning confusion, wastes spec effort, and risks building the same thing twice. Cleaning this up will clarify the true size of the backlog and make prioritisation decisions accurate.
recommended_actions.source: Feature Pipeline: Active list — [D P2] HMM Regime Detection, [D P2] Gaussian HMM Regime Classifier, [D P2] Hurst Exponent Per-Stock Regime Classifier, [D P2] Walk-Forward HMM Retraining Pipeline; [C P2] IC-Weighted Dynamic Signal Scorer and IC-Weighted Dynamic Signal Scoring; multiple cross-list duplicates
recommended_actions.cowork_prompt:
SESSION BRIEF: Deduplicate the Decifer feature pipeline to eliminate overlapping and duplicate entries.

CONTEXT: The active pipeline has 77 features and the backlog has 25. A review has identified multiple groups of near-duplicate entries that are creating planning confusion. The goal of this session is to consolidate each duplicate group into a single canonical spec entry.

DUPLICATE GROUPS TO RESOLVE:
1. HMM REGIME: '[D P2] Gaussian HMM Regime Classifier — 3-State SPY Volatility Filter', '[D P2] HMM Regime Detection — 3-state HMM on vol + RSI', '[D P2] HMM Regime Detection' (backlog), '[D P2] Walk-Forward HMM Retraining Pipeline', '[D P2] Regime-Conditioned Kelly Multiplier Table', '[D P2] Regime-Conditioned Position Size Scalar (VIX Regime + HMM Integration)' — consolidate into one HMM spec and one HMM-to-sizing integration spec.
2. IC WEIGHTING: '[C P2] IC-Weighted Dynamic Signal Scorer' and '[C P2] IC-Weighted Dynamic Signal Scoring' — these appear to be identical. Merge into one entry.
3. WALK-FORWARD: '[B P2] Walk-Forward Backtesting' and '[C P2] Walk-Forward Efficiency (WFE) Ratio Validator' — determine if these are sequential (WFE depends on WF backtesting) and if so, make one a dependency of the other rather than separate items.
4. CONNECTION MANAGER: '[B P2] Connection Manager — Multi-Account IBKR' (active) and '[MA1 P0] Connection Manager — Multi-Account IBKR' (backlog) — same feature, different priority labels. Resolve to one entry.
5. PER-ACCOUNT CONFIG: '[B P2] Per-Account Config Profiles' (active) and '[MA1 P0] Per-Account Config Profiles' (backlog) — same feature. Resolve to one entry.
6. REDDIT SIGNALS: '[B P2] Reddit Mention Velocity Dimension' and '[B P2] Reddit Mentions Surge Detector' and '[B P1] Sentiment Volume Change (SVC) Reddit Signal' — evaluate whether these are distinct enough to keep separate or should merge.
7. SENTIMENT DECAY: '[C P2] Alpha Decay Monitor — Per-Signal IC Rolling Health Check', '[C P2] Sentiment Decay Tracker with IC Rolling Health Check', '[B P1] Signal Decay Monitor & Freshness Dashboard' — likely three overlapping specs for the same capability.

OUTPUT: A cleaned pipeline list with duplicates removed and dependency relationships made explicit. Update the project tracking document.

recommended_actions.action: Build: Extract Signal Objects from Brain Loop — implement typed Signal objects and signal_pipeline.py contract fully
recommended_actions.why_now: This is tagged [B P1] in active AND [MA1 P0] in backlog, making it the highest-consensus priority item across both lists. The commit log shows 'refactor(pipeline): extract signal pipeline from bot.py into signal_pipeline.py' was done today, but the candle gate tests are failing which suggests the extraction is incomplete or the contract is not fully defined. Completing this cleanly is the foundation for the WebSocket Signal Broadcaster, the AccountDecisionAgent, and every multi-account feature — nothing in the MA1 milestone can proceed without typed Signal objects flowing through a stable pipeline.
recommended_actions.source: Pipeline: [B P1] Extract Signal Objects from Brain Loop (active); [MA1 P0] Extract Signal Objects from Brain Loop (backlog); Recent Commits: refactor(pipeline): extract signal pipeline from bot.py into signal_pipeline.py
recommended_actions.cowork_prompt:
SESSION BRIEF: Complete the typed Signal object extraction from bot.py in Decifer.

CONTEXT: Today's commit extracted the signal pipeline from bot.py into signal_pipeline.py. However, the candle gate tests are failing (suggesting the Signal object interface is not fully stable), and the MA1 milestone (multi-account) has this as a P0 prerequisite. The goal is to complete and harden the Signal object contract.

TASKS:
1. Review signal_pipeline.py as committed today. Define or confirm a typed Signal dataclass/namedtuple with at minimum: symbol (str), direction (Enum: LONG/SHORT/NEUTRAL), conviction_score (float 0-10), dimension_scores (dict[str, float]), regime (str), timestamp (datetime), source_agent_ids (list[str]), metadata (dict).
2. Ensure the candle gate reads from and writes to the Signal object correctly — this is likely why the candle gate tests are failing. Fix the interface mismatch.
3. Confirm bot.py no longer contains inline signal scoring logic — it should only call signal_pipeline.py and receive Signal objects back.
4. Add a Signal serialisation method (to_dict / to_json) that will be needed by the WebSocket Signal Broadcaster.
5. Write or update tests to cover: Signal object construction, direction assignment, serialisation round-trip, and gate application (candle gate, regime gate).
6. Confirm all 7 previously failing candle gate tests now pass.
7. Document the Signal object schema in a docstring or /docs/signal_schema.md — this will be referenced by every downstream feature.

recommended_actions.action: Promote research finding: IC-Weighted Dynamic Signal Scoring — move from research to a concrete, deduplicated spec and prioritise for build
recommended_actions.why_now: Two separate research entries from 2026-03-29 both confirm the same finding with high conviction: IC-weighted dynamic signal scoring substantially outperforms static equal-weight models (one study found significant outperformance, GPT-class models achieve 72-74% directional accuracy). Decifer currently uses equal-weight signal compositing. This is the highest-confidence research-to-alpha gap in the project. There are already two duplicate active pipeline entries for this feature — the housekeeping action will consolidate them, and this action ensures the consolidated spec is written with enough detail to build from immediately after the signal pipeline stabilises.
recommended_actions.source: Research: Signal Quality & Alpha Generation (2026-03-29, both entries); Pipeline: [C P2] IC-Weighted Dynamic Signal Scorer; [C P2] IC-Weighted Dynamic Signal Scoring
recommended_actions.cowork_prompt:
SESSION BRIEF: Write a complete build spec for IC-Weighted Dynamic Signal Scoring in Decifer.

CONTEXT: Two research findings from 2026-03-29 confirm that IC-weighted dynamic signal scoring substantially outperforms static equal-weight models. Decifer currently uses equal-weight compositing across its 10 signal dimensions. There are two duplicate pipeline entries for this feature — this session will produce one authoritative spec.

RESEARCH SUMMARY:
- Multi-factor signal models consistently outperform single-factor approaches only when factor weights are dynamically calibrated using Information Coefficient (IC)
- One 2025 study found IC-weighted models substantially outperform static equal-weight signal models
- IC = correlation between signal value and forward return, computed on a rolling basis

TASKS:
1. Write a single canonical spec for IC-Weighted Dynamic Signal Scoring that covers:
   a. How IC is computed: for each of the 10 signal dimensions, compute Spearman rank correlation between dimension score and 5-day forward return, rolling 60-day window, minimum 20 observations
   b. How weights are derived: IC values are normalised (softmax or proportional) to produce a weight vector summing to 1.0
   c. Noise floor: if IC for a dimension falls below 0.02 (or is not statistically significant), floor it to equal weight (1/10) rather than zero
   d. How the weighted composite replaces the current equal-weight composite in signal_pipeline.py
   e. Fallback: if insufficient history exists (< 20 trades), use equal weights
   f. Dashboard display: show current IC per dimension on the per-account dashboard
2. Define the data dependency: this feature requires completed trades with 5-day forward return data — confirm the trade journal captures this
3. Specify the test cases: weight vector sums to 1.0, noise floor is applied, weights update on new trade completion, fallback to equal weights works
4. Remove the duplicate entry from the pipeline and replace with this single spec
5. Tag the spec as [B P1] — it should be built immediately after the signal pipeline stabilises

recommended_actions.action: Build: Regime-Gated Momentum / Mean-Reversion Signal Router using the VIX-proxy regime detector already locked in
recommended_actions.why_now: The commit 'fix(arch): lock VIX-proxy as sole regime detector, defer HMM' confirms a regime detection architectural decision has been made today. The VIX-proxy regime is live. The research finding from 2026-03-30 confirms regime adaptation consistently outperforms static strategies. Three pipeline items directly build on this locked architecture: Regime-Gated Momentum / Mean-Reversion Signal Router [B P1], Regime-Conditional Signal Weighting [B P1], and Day-of-Week Session Filter [B P2]. The router should be built now while the architecture decision is fresh and before the HMM items create confusion about which regime source to use. This converts the already-running regime detector from a passive classifier into an active signal routing layer.
recommended_actions.source: Recent Commits: fix(arch): lock VIX-proxy as sole regime detector, defer HMM; Pipeline: [B P1] Regime-Gated Momentum / Mean-Reversion Signal Router; [B P1] Regime-Conditional Signal Weighting; Research: Market Regime Detection & Adaptation (2026-03-30)
recommended_actions.cowork_prompt:
SESSION BRIEF: Build the Regime-Gated Momentum / Mean-Reversion Signal Router in Decifer.

CONTEXT: Today's commit locked the VIX-proxy as the sole regime detector (deferring HMM). The regime detector is live and classifying the market into states (at minimum: BULL/BEAR/CHOPPY based on VIX levels — the scanner already uses CHOPPY regime per a recent fix commit). Research from 2026-03-30 confirms regime-adaptive systems consistently outperform static strategies. This session builds the signal routing layer that uses the regime output.

ARCHITECTURE:
- Regime source: VIX-proxy (already live, do not add HMM dependency)
- Regime states: confirm what states the current detector produces (likely BULL, BEAR, CHOPPY or similar)
- Router logic: in BULL/trending regime → upweight momentum dimensions (TREND, MOMENTUM, BREAKOUT), downweight mean-reversion dimensions (REVERSION); in CHOPPY/ranging regime → upweight REVERSION, downweight TREND/MOMENTUM; in BEAR → reduce overall position sizing, enable short scanning

TASKS:
1. Read the current regime detector output format from the codebase — confirm the state labels and where the regime is stored/published
2. In signal_pipeline.py, add a regime_weight_multiplier(dimension: str, regime: str) -> float function that returns a multiplier (e.g., 1.5 for favoured dimensions, 0.7 for disfavoured) based on the regime
3. Apply the multipliers to the raw dimension scores before compositing (after IC weighting if IC weighting is live, otherwise as the primary weighting layer)
4. Add a unit test: given regime=BULL, TREND dimension gets multiplier > 1.0; given regime=CHOPPY, REVERSION dimension gets multiplier > 1.0
5. Log the applied regime and multipliers at DEBUG level per scan cycle
6. Add a small dashboard indicator showing current regime and active routing mode
7. Confirm the scanner's existing CHOPPY regime momentum shorts handling is consistent with this router (per the 'fix(scanner): enable momentum shorts scan in CHOPPY regime' commit)

recommended_actions.action: Spec: Write the paper trading performance benchmark spec — define the exit criteria for paper_trading_single_account stage
recommended_actions.why_now: There is no documented definition of when Decifer is ready to move from paper_trading_single_account to live_trading_single_account. Without this, the team will either move to live trading too early (risking real capital on an unproven system) or never move (because there is no clear finish line). The vision explicitly states 'the core bot must first be profitable and reliable on paper' but 'profitable and reliable' is not quantified anywhere in the pipeline, backlog, or research. This spec is a prerequisite for every future stage-gate decision.
recommended_actions.source: Vision: 'The core bot must first be profitable and reliable on paper, then transition to live trading'; Stage map: paper_trading_single_account → live_trading_single_account; No existing spec covering stage exit criteria
recommended_actions.cowork_prompt:
SESSION BRIEF: Write the paper trading performance benchmark spec for Decifer — define the exit criteria for stage paper_trading_single_account.

CONTEXT: Decifer's vision states the bot must be 'profitable and reliable on paper' before moving to live trading. There is currently no documented definition of what that means in measurable terms. This session produces a spec that defines the stage exit criteria.

TASKS:
Write a spec document (docs/stage_exit_criteria.md or similar) that defines:

1. MINIMUM PAPER TRADING DURATION: How many calendar days / trading days of paper trading must complete before evaluation? (Suggested: minimum 60 calendar days / ~42 trading days to cover multiple market regimes)

2. PROFITABILITY THRESHOLD: What Sharpe ratio, win rate, and total return must be achieved? Suggested starting point based on practitioner standards:
   - Sharpe ratio > 1.0 (annualised, on paper trades)
   - Win rate > 52% (above random for a directional system)
   - Max drawdown < 15%
   - Profit factor > 1.3
   - Minimum 50 completed round-trip trades (statistical significance)

3. RELIABILITY THRESHOLD: What system health metrics must hold?
   - Uptime > 99% over the evaluation window
   - Zero unhandled exceptions causing missed trades
   - All 6 agents returning valid JSON on > 99% of calls
   - Order fill watcher completing on 100% of placed orders

4. SIGNAL QUALITY GATE: At least 3 of the 10 signal dimensions must show positive IC (> 0.02) over the evaluation window

5. MANUAL REVIEW CHECKLIST: List of things a human must review before approving stage advancement (e.g., spot-check 10 random trade decisions, confirm no data-snooping in backtests)

6. ROLLBACK CRITERIA: Define what would trigger a rollback from live to paper if live trading is started

Deliver a draft spec and flag any thresholds that need founder sign-off before being finalised.

recommended_actions.action: Build: Multi-Source Sentiment Consensus Gate [A P0] — the only P0 active pipeline item not yet shipped
recommended_actions.why_now: This is tagged [A P0] in the active pipeline — the highest priority category in the entire active list, and the only A-category P0 item not yet shipped. The research finding from 2026-03-31 explicitly states 'LLM-based sentiment scoring of financial news generates statistically significant alpha, with GPT-class models achieving 72-74% directional accuracy.' The Claude Sentiment Scoring Pipeline and Claude Earnings Call Scorer are already in the active pipeline as dependencies. A consensus gate that requires multiple sentiment sources to agree before allowing trade entry is a high-signal alpha filter that directly addresses false positive reduction — the most important metric at the paper trading stage.
recommended_actions.source: Pipeline: [A P0] Multi-Source Sentiment Consensus Gate; Research: Sentiment Analysis & NLP for Trading (2026-03-31)
recommended_actions.cowork_prompt:
SESSION BRIEF: Build the Multi-Source Sentiment Consensus Gate in Decifer — [A P0] pipeline item.

CONTEXT: This is the only A-category P0 item in the active pipeline. Research from 2026-03-31 confirms LLM-based sentiment scoring achieves 72-74% directional accuracy. The gate adds a consensus-gating layer to trade execution that checks whether news sentiment agrees with the technical signal before allowing order placement.

FEATURE DESCRIPTION (from pipeline):
Add a consensus-gating layer to Decifer's trade execution that checks whether news sentiment agrees before a trade is entered. If sentiment is strongly opposed to the trade direction, block the entry.

TASKS:
1. Review what sentiment sources are currently available in the codebase (Yahoo RSS, any existing Claude scoring, FinBERT if present). Inventory what is live vs what is planned.
2. Define the gate contract: gate takes a Signal object (direction=LONG or SHORT) and returns PASS/BLOCK with a reason string. BLOCK if: (a) primary sentiment score opposes the trade direction with confidence > threshold, OR (b) no sentiment data is available within the last N hours (configurable, default 4h)
3. Implement the gate as a standalone function/class in a sentiment_gate.py module (or within signal_pipeline.py if that is the established pattern)
4. Wire the gate into signal_pipeline.py so that Signals with BLOCK sentiment are downgraded to NEUTRAL direction and do not proceed to order placement
5. If Claude sentiment scoring is already partially implemented, connect it here. If not, use a placeholder that returns PASS with a 'no_sentiment_data' flag so the gate is structurally complete even before sentiment sources are live
6. Add configuration knobs: sentiment_gate_enabled (bool), sentiment_gate_threshold (float, default 0.6), sentiment_lookback_hours (int, default 4)
7. Write tests: BLOCK when sentiment opposes direction above threshold, PASS when sentiment agrees, PASS when no data and gate is in permissive mode
8. Log all gate decisions at INFO level with the sentiment score and source

recommended_actions.action: Decision: Determine whether the Alpha Decay Chart [B P1] and Signal Decay Monitor [B P1] are the same feature or different features, and resolve the overlap with IC-Weighted Dynamic Signal Scoring
recommended_actions.why_now: There are three P1 pipeline items that all appear to track signal quality decay over time: 'Alpha Decay Chart — Per-Trade Forward Return Distribution Tracker', 'Alpha Decay Monitor — Per-Signal IC Rolling Health Check', and 'Signal Decay Monitor & Freshness Dashboard'. IC-Weighted Dynamic Signal Scoring also requires rolling IC computation. If these are built as separate features without a decision, the codebase will have multiple redundant IC-tracking implementations — a maintainability problem in a 25,000-line codebase that is already growing fast. This decision gates the build order for all four items.
recommended_actions.source: Pipeline: [B P1] Alpha Decay Chart; [C P2] Alpha Decay Monitor; [B P1] Signal Decay Monitor & Freshness Dashboard; [C P2] IC-Weighted Dynamic Signal Scorer; Code Health: largest modules include signals.py (1691L), ml_engine.py (969L) — complexity risk
recommended_actions.cowork_prompt:
SESSION BRIEF: Make and document the architectural decision on signal quality tracking — consolidate Alpha Decay Chart, Alpha Decay Monitor, Signal Decay Monitor, and IC-Weighted Signal Scoring into a unified design.

CONTEXT: The Decifer pipeline has four separate items that all involve tracking how well signal dimensions predict forward returns over time:
1. [B P1] Alpha Decay Chart — Per-Trade Forward Return Distribution Tracker: tracks forward return distribution per trade to detect when alpha is decaying
2. [C P2] Alpha Decay Monitor — Per-Signal IC Rolling Health Check: rolling IC per signal dimension to detect decay
3. [B P1] Signal Decay Monitor & Freshness Dashboard: weekly process computing rolling IC per dimension with dashboard display
4. [C P2] IC-Weighted Dynamic Signal Scoring: uses rolling IC to dynamically weight signal dimensions

All four require the same underlying data: (signal dimension score at entry, forward return at N days). Building them separately will result in duplicate data pipelines.

TASKS:
1. Map the four features against a single data model: one trade record should contain (symbol, entry_date, dimension_scores[10], direction, 1d_forward_return, 5d_forward_return, 10d_forward_return)
2. Decide: should there be a single SignalQualityTracker module that (a) stores the above data, (b) computes rolling IC per dimension, (c) exposes IC values for the dynamic weighter, and (d) exposes decay metrics for the dashboard — or should these remain separate?
3. Recommended decision: YES — build one SignalQualityTracker. Document the decision and the module interface.
4. Confirm the trade journal already captures or can be extended to capture forward returns (this may require a scheduled lookback job that fills in forward returns N days after entry)
5. Replace the four separate pipeline entries with: (a) SignalQualityTracker core module [P1], (b) IC-Weighted Scoring integration [P1], (c) Dashboard display [P2]
6. Document the decision in a lightweight architecture decision record (ADR) in /docs/adr/

recommended_actions.action: Validate: Confirm drawdown brake, HWM persistence, and ATR trailing stop are behaving correctly in paper trading simulation
recommended_actions.why_now: Three critical risk management features were shipped in the last 24 hours: drawdown brake (flatten all on breach + persist HWM across restarts), ATR trailing stop (slide stop up as HWM advances), and capital-agnostic daily loss limit. These are the primary capital protection mechanisms for paper trading. If any of these has a logic error — wrong HWM initialisation, incorrect ATR calculation, drawdown brake triggering on the wrong equity value — it will either fail to protect capital when needed or over-trigger and block legitimate trades. Given the speed of the sprint, a targeted validation pass is essential before relying on these features for paper trading assessment.
recommended_actions.source: Recent Commits: feat(risk): drawdown brake — flatten all on breach + persist HWM across restarts; feat(risk): ATR trailing stop — slide stop up as HWM advances; feat(risk): capital-agnostic daily loss limit + PDT-aware mode; Vision: reliability requirement before live trading
recommended_actions.cowork_prompt:
SESSION BRIEF: Validate the three new risk management features shipped in the last 24 hours in Decifer.

CONTEXT: Three risk management features were shipped today in rapid succession. Before paper trading results can be trusted for the stage-gate evaluation, these must be verified to work correctly.

FEATURES TO VALIDATE:
1. Drawdown Brake (risk.py): 'flatten all on breach + persist HWM across restarts'
2. ATR Trailing Stop (risk.py): 'slide stop up as HWM advances'
3. Capital-Agnostic Daily Loss Limit + PDT-aware mode (risk.py)

TASKS:

DRAWDOWN BRAKE:
1. Find the drawdown brake implementation in risk.py. Confirm: (a) HWM is loaded from persistent storage on startup (not reset to current equity), (b) drawdown % is calculated as (HWM - current_equity) / HWM, (c) the flatten-all trigger fires at the correct threshold (check config), (d) after flatten, the brake prevents new entries until reset condition is met
2. Write or find the test for drawdown brake — confirm there is a test that simulates equity falling below the threshold and verifies positions are flattened
3. Simulate an edge case: bot restarts mid-drawdown. Confirm HWM is correctly restored and brake remains active

ATR TRAILING STOP:
1. Find the ATR trailing stop in risk.py. Confirm: (a) stop level only moves UP (never down) for long positions, (b) ATR is recalculated at appropriate frequency (per bar, not per tick), (c) stop is correctly attached to the position object and monitored by the order fill watcher
2. Check: what happens if ATR calculation fails (e.g., insufficient bars)? Is there a fallback?

DAILY LOSS LIMIT:
1. Confirm 'capital-agnostic' means the limit is expressed as a % of current equity, not a fixed dollar amount
2. Confirm PDT-aware mode correctly counts day trades (< 3 round trips in 5 days for accounts < $25k)
3. Verify the daily loss limit resets at market open each day

OUTPUT: A validation checklist with pass/fail per item and any bugs found.

product_roadmap.goal: Achieve a stable, fully tested signal and risk pipeline that produces trustworthy paper trading results
product_roadmap.rationale: The system cannot be evaluated for stage advancement or trusted for live trading until the test suite is clean, the signal pipeline contract is defined, and the new risk features are verified. All further feature work builds on this foundation.
features: Fix agent JSON parse errors — typed NullRecommendation fallback
Validate drawdown brake, ATR trailing stop, daily loss limit behaviour
Write paper trading stage exit criteria spec
product_roadmap.exit_criteria: Test suite at 100% pass rate, agent parse errors handled without data loss, 10-dimension signal producing non-degenerate scores, risk features verified by simulation, stage exit criteria documented and founder-approved

product_roadmap.goal: Implement dynamic IC-weighted signal scoring, sentiment consensus gate, and regime-gated signal routing to measurably improve signal quality
product_roadmap.rationale: Research confirms these three capabilities have the highest evidence-backed alpha contribution. They must be built before the paper trading evaluation window starts so that the evaluation tests the best version of the signal engine, not an interim version.
features: Regime-Gated Momentum / Mean-Reversion Signal Router [B P1]
SignalQualityTracker core module (consolidates Alpha Decay + Signal Decay + IC tracking)
Options Flow Proxy Signal via Put/Call Ratio [B P1]
product_roadmap.exit_criteria: Sentiment gate blocking at least some trades, regime router directing signal weights by regime, IC weights updating after each batch of completed trades, rolling IC per dimension visible on dashboard

product_roadmap.goal: Run 60+ days of paper trading against the fully-featured signal engine and evaluate against the stage exit criteria
product_roadmap.rationale: Only after the signal pipeline is complete and stable does it make sense to run a formal evaluation. Running the clock earlier would require restarting the evaluation after each major signal change.
features: Slippage & Execution Cost Model for Backtester
product_roadmap.exit_criteria: 60 days completed, all stage exit criteria met (Sharpe > 1.0, win rate > 52%, max DD < 15%, > 50 trades, 3+ dimensions with positive IC), manual review checklist signed off

product_roadmap.goal: Transition from paper to live trading on a single account with strict risk controls and monitoring
product_roadmap.rationale: Live trading introduces execution risk, slippage, and real capital. Must be preceded by passing paper evaluation and must include live-specific safeguards.
features: Live vs paper mode toggle with capital limits
product_roadmap.exit_criteria: 30 days live trading with live P&L matching paper trading within expected slippage bounds, no unhandled exceptions, drawdown brake never triggered without correct intervention

product_roadmap.goal: Move the bot from local to cloud VPS with Docker, automated restarts, and web dashboard
product_roadmap.rationale: Cloud hosting is required before multi-user can be considered. Single-account cloud validates the deployment architecture.
features: Cloud VPS Setup and Deployment [B P2]
Reconnection Logic — Backoff, Sequence, State Sync
Parameterise account_id Through Orders and Risk
product_roadmap.exit_criteria: Bot runs unattended on VPS for 2 weeks with < 1 unplanned restart, web dashboard accessible remotely, reconnection logic tested by simulated network drop

product_roadmap.goal: Enable friends and family to connect their own IBKR accounts through a web interface
product_roadmap.rationale: This is the target state described in the vision. All prior phases must complete first.
features: Extract Signal Objects — WebSocket Signal Broadcaster
Per-Account Risk Profile Config Schema with Multi-Tier Presets
product_roadmap.exit_criteria: At least 3 external users connected with their own IBKR accounts, each receiving independent signals and risk controls, web dashboard functional for all users, no cross-account data leakage

risks.risk: Agent JSON parse failures (agents.py:483) cause silent consensus failures during paper trading, producing invalid trade decisions that are never logged or flagged
risks.mitigation: Implement typed NullRecommendation fallback immediately (Action #1). Add a metric counter for parse failures exposed on the dashboard. Alert if parse failure rate exceeds 1% in any rolling 100-call window.

risks.risk: The 10-dimension signal refactor (alpha-pipeline-v2) may have introduced a scoring regression — if one dimension is broken or zero-contributing, all paper trade results during the evaluation window will be invalid
risks.mitigation: Run validation session (Action #2) before the evaluation window clock starts. Do not count paper trading days toward the stage exit criteria until the signal validation passes.

risks.risk: 70 failing tests in a 1,332-test suite represents a 5% failure rate. As the codebase grows (currently 25,730 lines, 37 modules), unaddressed test failures compound and create coverage blind spots in critical execution paths
risks.mitigation: Prioritise test fixes (Action #1). Institute a policy: no new features merged with a net increase in failing test count. Add CI gate that blocks merges if failures increase.

risks.risk: The active pipeline has 77 items with no stage-gating — features planned for multi-user production (Docker containers, per-user auth) are mixed with features needed now for paper trading, creating prioritisation confusion and risk of building out-of-sequence
risks.mitigation: Complete the deduplication housekeeping (Action #3) and tag every pipeline item with its required stage (paper/live/cloud/multi-user). Freeze all items tagged cloud or later until Phase 4.

risks.risk: bot.py is 3,140 lines — the largest module. Despite today's signal_pipeline.py extraction, the main loop is still very large. Further features added directly to bot.py will make it untestable and unmaintainable
risks.mitigation: Enforce the Signal object extraction contract (Action #4) as a hard boundary: bot.py orchestrates, signal_pipeline.py generates signals, risk.py manages risk. No signal scoring logic in bot.py. Set a soft line limit of 3,500 for bot.py and flag if exceeded.

risks.risk: No paper trading performance benchmark exists — the team has no defined finish line for paper trading. This risks either premature live trading (capital risk) or perpetual paper trading (opportunity cost)
risks.mitigation: Write the stage exit criteria spec (Action #6) and get founder sign-off on the thresholds before the evaluation window starts.

risks.risk: The HMM regime detection has been deferred (per today's architecture decision commit) but 4+ HMM pipeline items remain active and unresolved, creating a planning debt that will resurface and potentially reverse the architecture decision
risks.mitigation: In the deduplication housekeeping session (Action #3), move all HMM items to a single 'HMM — Deferred' backlog entry with a clear condition for reconsideration (e.g., 'revisit if VIX-proxy regime shows IC < 0.05 after 60 days'). Document the deferral decision as an ADR.

risks.risk: The drawdown brake 'flatten all on breach' may interact badly with the ATR trailing stop — if both fire simultaneously on a gap-down, the order of operations may attempt to send conflicting orders to IBKR
risks.mitigation: Include order-of-operations test in the risk feature validation (Action #10): simulate a scenario where both drawdown brake and ATR stop trigger in the same scan cycle. Confirm the system sends one consistent set of close orders, not duplicates.

risks.risk: The pipeline contains multiple sentiment features (Claude Sentiment, FinBERT, Yahoo RSS, SEC Form 4, Reddit SVC, Google Trends, SEC 10-K) but no clear architecture for how they combine — risk of building 7 separate sentiment pipelines that are never unified
risks.mitigation: The Multi-Source Sentiment Consensus Gate (Action #8) should be designed as the unifying interface. All sentiment sources should return a standardised SentimentSignal object that the gate consumes. Write this interface contract before building individual sources.

risks.risk: No user-facing product exists yet (no web UI, no auth, no onboarding) — all 5 UI/auth backlog items are tagged MA3 which is 3 milestones away. If the technical bot proves profitable but the product layer is underdeveloped, the multi-user beta launch will be blocked by months of UI work
risks.mitigation: Begin a lightweight web dashboard spec (already in pipeline as [B P2] Web Dashboard) during Phase 3 (paper evaluation) so that UI development can run in parallel with live trading validation rather than sequentially after it.

roadmap_gaps: 1. PAPER TRADING EVALUATION FRAMEWORK: There is no spec anywhere for how paper trading results will be collected, analysed, and used to make the go/no-go decision for live trading. The exit criteria spec (Action #6) addresses this but it is a significant gap that has no pipeline entry. 2. IBKR PAPER ACCOUNT CONFIGURATION: There is no spec covering how the IBKR paper account is configured, what starting capital is used, how market data subscriptions are handled, or how the paper account differs from a live account in terms of order handling. This is a prerequisite operational detail with no tracking. 3. ERROR ALERTING AND OPERATIONAL MONITORING: Beyond the dashboard, there is no spec for how the founder is alerted to system failures (unhandled exceptions, connection drops, drawdown brake triggers, agent parse failure spikes) during unattended operation. Telegram bot covers trade notifications but not system health alerts. 4. DATA PIPELINE RESILIENCE: There is no spec for what happens when external data sources fail (yfinance rate limit, Yahoo RSS down, IBKR data gap). The system likely has implicit dependencies on these that are untested for failure modes. 5. BACKTESTING DATA MANAGEMENT: There is no spec for historical data storage, caching, or management. The backtester presumably downloads data on demand — this is fragile for walk-forward validation over long windows. 6. POSITION SIZING AUDIT TRAIL: There is no spec for logging why a specific position size was chosen (Kelly output, ATR cap, VIX adjustment, regime scalar) — this is essential for debugging unexpected sizing behaviour in live trading. 7. SECRET / CREDENTIAL MANAGEMENT: There is no spec for how IBKR credentials, API keys (Claude, etc.) are stored and rotated — this will be critical before cloud deployment and is not addressed in any pipeline item beyond a passing mention in the Docker containers spec. 8. REGULATORY AND COMPLIANCE CONSIDERATIONS: No mention anywhere of PDT rule management beyond a config flag, wash sale rule awareness, or any compliance logging that would be needed for users managing real accounts.
